data.title: HiPEAC Vision, a Blueprint for European Survival
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

How Europe’s Chips Act 2.0 Must Embrace the ‘Next Computing Paradigm’

The European Union’s ambitious drive for technological sovereignty is entering a critical second phase. With the original Chips Act having successfully mobilized capital—total commitments reached nearly €69 billion—the subsequent phase, Chips Act 2.0, must fundamentally shift focus from headline-grabbing factory commitments to securing the next generation of computing architecture and talent. 

This strategic pivot finds its intellectual anchor in the HiPEAC Vision 2025, a roadmap arguing that Europe’s future relevance depends not merely on manufacturing silicon wafers, but on mastering the distributed, sustainable computing paradigm that Artificial Intelligence (AI) now demands.

This recalibration is made urgent by the reality of the U.S. “Great Reallocation,” where aggressive trade policy and massive subsidies are pulling European corporate champions, their capital, and their intellectual property across the Atlantic, straining Europe’s indigenous industrial base.

From centralized cloud to distributed intelligence

The HiPEAC Vision, setting out a course for European computing research over the next decade, centers on the concept of the Next Computing Paradigm (NCP). The NCP represents a crucial convergence of the computing continuum, spanning high-performance exascale computing, cloud data centers, and embedded devices, redefining how systems interact. HiPEAC views this shift as a dynamic collection of “federated and distributed services” that operate across this continuum.

Marc Duranton, the HiPEAC Vision editor-in-chief, argues the EU needs this blueprint because European computing technology companies have “lagged behind.” At the same time, counterparts in the United States and China have grown “stratospherically.” 

The NCP, infused with the exponential progress of AI, is specifically designed to leverage Europe’s strengths, such as its robust capacity to develop “edge and on-premise devices,” complex cyber-physical systems (CPS), and industrial automation tools. 

Crucially, the short-term recommendations propose using the emergence of “distributed agentic AI” to lay the groundwork for the NCP technology, serving as a blueprint for a more generic and omnipresent system. This model emphasizes local processing and federation, aiming to reduce reliance on centralized, foreign hyper-scalers for critical data security and infrastructure needs.

The EU Chips Act crossroads

Every single member state of the EU is rallying together in a bold Dutch-led initiative to strengthen the EU’s Chips Act. This groundbreaking effort, called the “Semicon Coalition,” was launched in March 2025 by the Netherlands, in collaboration with eight other forward-thinking member states.

While the first phase of the Chips Act successfully attracted foreign investment commitments, including major projects in Germany and France, a deep-dive assessment shows “structural limitations that threaten its long-term efficacy.” 

Furthermore, the current framework is heavily skewed toward “First-of-a-Kind” (FOAK) facilities, leaving the broader supply chain—design firms, equipment manufacturers, and key materials suppliers—undersupported.

Industry sentiment remains skeptical of the EU’s core target: seizing 20% of the global semiconductor market by 2030. According to a SEMI Europe consultation, “90% of respondent members deem this target unachievable,” citing a core disconnect: the lack of assured market demand in Europe remains “a primary deterrent to investment.”

To remedy this policy flaw, SEMI Europe advocates for a fundamental shift in the incentive toolbox. They strongly recommend that the Chips Act 2.0 mandate that Member States adopt a framework for “harmonized tax credits for semiconductor R&D and CAPEX.” 

“Our findings underline the need for a more resilient supply chain and forward-looking Chips Act that supports innovation and front-end manufacturing to further strengthen Europe’s ecosystem with focus on materials and equipment suppliers, design and advanced packaging to ensure long-term resilient technological sovereignty,” said Laith Altimime, President, SEMI Europe.

Additionally, tax credits offer “predictability and lower administrative overhead” compared to complex grants and are particularly effective for supporting Small and Medium-sized Enterprises (SMEs) and upgrading legacy facilities. Aligning with the HiPEAC vision, investing in the SME ecosystem is vital, as SMEs drive disruptive innovation.

The U.S. capital magnet

The urgency for a cohesive European strategy is heightened by aggressive U.S. industrial policy. Following the enactment of the “Genesis Mission” executive order and the threat of a “100% tariff on imported semiconductors,” global technology players are reshoring billions of dollars in infrastructure investment to the United States, creating a palpable “capital drain” from Europe.

European industrial champions have been compelled to participate in this Great Reallocation. Nokia, for instance, recently pledged a $4 billion investment in the U.S. This commitment marks a pivot for the Finnish telecom giant, shifting R&D toward “optical chips”—silicon photonics—to supply the internal wiring of new AI data clusters, positioning the company as a “trusted” supplier to the U.S. security state. 

Similarly, Ericsson expanded its smart factory in Texas to ensure compliance with federal procurement rules. Yet, even as they comply, European corporate values clash with American industrial realities; Ericsson’s internal mandate that contractors adhere to “Net Zero” climate goals has “triggered a backlash” from U.S. contractors, exposing the tension between European environmental, social, and governance (ESG) goals and the industrial labor market.

The broader geopolitical consequence is clear: the U.S. has weaponized its deregulation. The combination of massive U.S. incentives and threats has forced the European Union to reconsider its regulatory landscape, with reports indicating that EU officials are considering delaying the rollout of the high-risk rules under the AI Act for “up to 16 months,” effectively “pausing regulation to prevent further capital flight.”

Sovereign future by securing chip design

To avoid becoming merely a consumer market for technology designed and manufactured elsewhere, Chips Act 2.0 must adopt the philosophical underpinnings of the HiPEAC Vision: prioritize the design and toolchain ecosystem.

The establishment of the Chips Joint Undertaking (Chips JU) to replace the KDT JU aims to address this, with objectives focused on building advanced design capacities and establishing a Virtual Design Platform (VDP). 

This VDP could “simplify and accelerate the process of going ‘from the lab to the fab'” for SMEs and startups, democratizing access to complex design tools. The effort must accelerate through the support of crucial technologies championed by HiPEAC, such as chiplets and open-source hardware like RISC-V, which lower barriers to entry and reduce dependence on dominant non-EU manufacturers.

By anchoring its strategy in the distributed intelligence and sustainability focus articulated by HiPEAC, and by implementing the industrial support mechanisms advocated by SEMI, Europe can evolve the Chips Act from an emergency measure into a coherent, long-term industrial strategy. This transition secures Europe’s position not just as a location for foreign factories, but as a sovereign architect of the next digital future.

data.title: Q.ANT Raises Series A, Debuts Second-Gen TFLN Photonic Chip
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Photonic chip startup Q.ANT closed a series A funding round, bringing its total funding to $80 million, as well as announced a new four-year partnership with the Jülich Supercomputing Centre to explore possible applications of its technology. The German company also unveiled a second-generation of its chip, the Q.ANT NPU 2, and an updated software stack that enables training on its chip.

Q.ANT is not optimizing for LLM inference acceleration, Q.ANT CEO Michael Förtsch told EE Times, preferring to focus on what will come next.

“A lot of work and a lot of money have been deployed into the execution of large language models,” Förtsch said. “We know we can contribute to this, but we feel that while a lot of economic investments have been made, the return on investment is still missing.”

More complicated neural network architectures, like image, audio and video networks, are still struggling to achieve scale, so this is where Q.ANT will focus, Förtsch added.  

“We’re pursuing a vision that we can have with our gen 3, a system that allows for real time inferencing of a 4k picture at a frame rate of 100 hertz per second, so that you really can realize AI based video streaming,” he said. “This seems to be where the CMOS world is falling short, not only in performance and computational density, but also with energy consumption.”

TFLN process

Q.ANT’s chips are based on thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN), specifically, with devices based on a 600-nm thick lithium niobate on insulator layer. TFLN’s non-linear optical properties allow natural acceleration for non-linear mathematics, saving energy in the data center.

The company’s 90-nm production line for its TFLN-based photonic compute chips at the Institute of Microelectronics in Stuttgart, Germany, has been up and running since early this year.

First-generation chips from this production line, and their servers, are available in the cloud for demonstration purposes, Förtsch said.

At SC 2025 this month, Q.ANT demonstrated its first-generation NPU running a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) to learn the image and reconstruct it versus a multi-layer perceptron (more like mainstream AI) running on a CPU. The KAN has around half the number of parameters versus the MLP. The NPU, running at 200 MHz, produced a better image (closer to the original data) than the CPU running at 2.4 GHz with the same number of training epochs in a similar time frame.

“We did this on a variety of pictures and the result is usually the same,” Förtsch said. “We use many fewer parameters to describe the model, because we have access to these non-linear equation systems, and the end result is always closer to the ground truth.”

Second generation

Q.ANT’s second-generation chip is more performant than its first; 8 GOPS versus the 1 MOPS of the first generation, but Förtsch is careful to point out that these operations are more complex than multiply-accumulates and so cannot be compared to performance figures from standard deep learning accelerators. Clock speed has been increased from 200 MHz to 2 GHz, and the NPU consumes around 150 W during operation.

The chip has eight individual channels (these are separate channels operated in parallel). Q.ANT has test chips with more channels, but the optics are limited by digital parts of the system like memory, ADCs and DACs, Förtsch said.

“With eight channels, we were comfortable the yield on our production line was sufficient for a stable process,” he said.

Software work has enabled eight Q.ANT PCIe cards (one accelerator chip per card) to be driven by a single CPU host.

The company’s model zoo includes new algorithms and network architectures, developed in house to get the maximum benefit out of Q.ANT’s hardware.

“It’s a smarter algorithm set, because it allows for using much less data to get the same result, or even a better result,” Förtsch said. “It also matches the capability of the processor and looks at fundamental functions like sine, cosine, convolution, Fourier transform, and so on, which are strong on our processor, and then maps the algorithms with respect to the strongest primitives of the NPU.”

Most applications already call functions from libraries. For example, for a Fourier transform, the library would decompose that into a set of hardware-specific linear equations, which is then written to machine code, then pushed down to the hardware. With Q.ANT’s non-linear capabilities, a Fourier transform can be pushed straight to the hardware as a primitive.

“The conversion process is much easier because you push whatever you want to execute directly to the processor, you don’t need to translate it into simpler maths,” Förtsch said. “AI actually has beautiful math, but the way we currently execute it on the processor is brute force, it’s not elegant.”

Customer trials

Q.ANT’s new four-year partnership with the Jülich Supercomputing Centre (JSC) at Forschungszentrum Jülich will explore the application possibilities for photonic computing, and the integration of photonic computing with classical computing. As part of the deal, JSC will buy a Q.ANT server.

An existing customer, the Leibniz Supercomputing Center (LSC), already has three Q.ANT servers with a mix of first- and second-generation hardware stood up. As lead customer, LSC was first to receive a software update enabling non-linear math on Q.ANT chips, and the customer is characterizing performance of Q.ANT’s second-generation chips currently, Fortsch said.

Q.ANT servers equipped with the company’s second-generation processor are expected to ship in the first half of 2026.

data.title: Mastiska Raises $10M Seed For Sovereign AI Chip Play in UAE
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

UAE-based Mastiṣka has raised a seed round of $10 million, mainly from sovereign wealth funds in the GCC, to build data-center class inference accelerators. The startup will use the funding to develop its technology and build a UAE-based fabless semiconductor company enabled by open-source technologies, Mastiṣka CEO Suresh Sugumar told EE Times.

U.S. regulations are forcing some countries to choose between U.S. and Chinese chip suppliers for AI compute, Sugumar said.

“I want to change that,” he said. “[When I started the company], I had a hard time convincing people to come on board, they tend to think sovereign AI is about data, but now people appreciate it.”

Sovereign AI starts with sovereign silicon, Sugumar said, noting that this is nevertheless challenging in a country without a history of chip design. Mastiṣka’s team of around 40 is divided between a model creation team in Abu Dhabi, and a VLSI team in India.

On the plus side, sovereign chip efforts are so important in their own right that the commercial pressures of developing new technologies are less relevant, Sugumar said.

“I’m not in a race to say I’m better than you,” he said. “I’m in a blue ocean where no matter what happens in the U.S. or China it won’t impact me to the extent that I should be worried about competition. People here understand that as long as we have a good enough product, we will build it from there.”

Sovereign customers will be given full access to audit the design for cybersecurity purposes, he added.

Some sovereign seed round investors came with strings: one had requirements to open a 60-person office in their GCC country. Sugumar is only too happy to accept deals of this type.

“As a byproduct, [the country] will also learn to be productive in this AI-centric world,” he said. “This way, they have the feeling that we designed it, our people designed it, and we are using it, that gives them comfort and pride, plus PR bragging rights.”

Potential sovereign customers outside of the UAE are other GCC countries, countries in South East Asia including India, and other BRICS and global South countries. Notable exceptions are the U.S. and China, who already have their own competitive sovereign chip programs, Sugumar said.  

FPGA product

Mastiṣka’s first products will be custom FPGA cards loaded with the company’s IP. These custom PCIe cards will feature the Agilex-7M augmented with up to 96 GB HBM (Altera’s own Agilex-7M cards come with up to 32 GB of HMB). This is not just for prototyping – this will be a commercially deployed product, Sugumar said.

The company’s FPGA-based demo can run DeepSeek-7B, albeit “pretty slowly,” Sugumar said.

“It will be a low-performance product—inference doesn’t require the latest and greatest high-performance GPUs,” Sugumar said. “But if I need 16 GPUs for a job, maybe I don’t mind using 32 FPGAs for the same job. There is a compromise—more rack space and other things—but this is the way to start.”

Mastiṣka will be working on taping out its own RISC-V-based ASIC in the next couple of years. The concept is neuromorphic-inspired digital utilizing parallelization.

“Quite a good amount of open-source technologies will be used, we will not share it back, but we’ll keep it with us,” Sugumar said.

Mastiṣka also has a model team that is working on brain-inspired models, including “modified transformers” that can rapidly improve performance and energy efficiency.

“The software team’s mission is to enable CUDA compatibility on our hardware,” Sugumar said. “That means you’re removing the barrier to shifting [from Nvidia hardware].”

Mastiṣka aims to tape out in the next three years or so, in order to give its IP time to mature, Sugumar added.

“We will make revenue [almost immediately] because we’ll be selling FPGA based servers,” he said. “That lets us patiently execute the chip design, because it’s really, really complex.”

data.title: Silicon Labs Targets India’s IoT Engineers with Studio 6 Overhaul
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Silicon Labs intends to expand its focus on India’s engineering base with the launch of its redesigned development environment, Simplicity Studio 6, built around Visual Studio Code.

At the Works With 2025 conference in Bangalore, India, Silicon Labs’ executives said that the latest update makes each step of IoT development simpler, from setup to debugging, with faster workflows for India’s resource-constrained design teams—all while still giving existing projects a stable path forward as customers rethink how they build connected devices. The company expects continued interest from both large design firms and smaller embedded teams in India.

EE Times gained additional insights from the company’s senior VP of software development, Manish Kothari, who said the company sees potential in partnering with maker spaces, university labs and open-source groups in India.

“We see broad opportunities in partnering with India’s developer communities. The goal is to help developers through training, documentation and easier access to tools,” Kothari said.

The updated ecosystem requires no heavy integrated development environments or large downloads, offering a command-line interface that supports full automation on low-end systems, aimed at startups, universities and incubators. Highlighting the company’s ongoing engagement with innovation hubs in India and student competitions, Kothari said, “These ecosystems often become early adopters of embedded tools because they experiment faster and try new workflows before industry uptake.”

Why Studio 5 needed a reset

Kothari said the company’s approach to migration from Studio 5 to Studio 6 is intentional. He clarified that customers on older releases do not need to move to the latest version.

“The company has a tested migration plan, and we are guiding customers through it. Studio 6 is backward compatible, but we want to limit the fan-out until all paths are validated. Older projects receiving only maintenance updates do not need to shift, and most customers have not asked to,” he said.

Studio 6’s integration with Visual Studio Code is what separates it from Studio 5. Ross Sabolcik, senior VP and general manager for Industrial and Commercial IoT products at Silicon Labs, said the tool chain is now componentized and available as plug-ins for Visual Studio Code.

“Developers can build and debug inside Visual Studio Code. They can also take tools like the network analyzer and use them as plug-ins in their preferred environment without switching context,” Sabolcik said.

Software-led differentiation, not a shift into services

Silicon Labs’ VP of Marketing and Sales, John Dixon, said the latest announcements do not mean the company is shifting into manufacturing services but instead aiming “to become the preferred development partner for IoT products …. we view hardware and software as a single product experience.”

Large-scale deployments, such as energy metering and retail shelf-labeling, have risen, increasing the pressure on platforms to maintain long-term reliability without requiring developers to rebuild connectivity foundations. Highlighting a shift among customers from hardware to software over the past decade, Dixon said, “We’ve removed complexity, provided example code and production-ready templates, and offered the infrastructure needed so engineers can focus on differentiation rather than on basic connectivity tasks.”

Kothari added that software plays a central role in customer retention. “Whether migrating across studio ecosystems, SDK releases, or device generations,” he said. “The goal is to make the process seamless. The upcoming Series 2 to Series 3 migration will be AI-assisted, with announcements expected next year.”

Where AI fits into Studio 6

As devices grow more capable, Kothari said customers now expect AI to help with code reorganization, configuration checks and migration because project scale and complexity have grown. He expects edge AI to become routine in IoT design, whether for diagnostics, documentation on the device or streamlining common development tasks.

The Simplicity AI SDK claims to understand project context—hardware, SDK version and wireless stack—and offers suggestions to trace configuration issues and optimize setups. “The intent is to accelerate workflows, not replace engineers,” Kothari clarified.

Initial versions of the AI SDK will be cloud-connected, though Silicon Labs is exploring offline or hybrid options for developers who require local operation. Beta feedback, especially from Indian teams, will focus on multi-protocol integrations, automation and cross-team workflows.

As Kothari reiterated, the goal is to help developers focus on differentiation rather than day-to-day complexity. “Studio 6 is Silicon Labs’ attempt to make that possible for global IoT teams and India’s fast-growing engineering community,” he emphasized.

See also:

Silicon Labs’ Matt Johnson: An Inflection Point for Edge AI in IoT Devices

Silicon Labs’ Wireless SoC Improves IoT Security with PSA Level 4 Certification

data.title: A Power Reshuffle Follows GF, TSMC GaN Tie-Up
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

TSMC’s vow to phase out its GaN fabrication in two years continues to make waves, and the latest episode of this technology saga relates to GlobalFoundries (GF) licensing TSMC’s 650-V and 80-V GaN power semiconductor manufacturing technologies.

TSMC played a pivotal role in advancing the commercialization of GaN-on-silicon (GaN-on-Si) technology by kickstarting the formal development of GaN manufacturing technologies in 2011 while leveraging its expertise in CMOS manufacturing. In 2015, TSMC began mass production of its GaN-on-Si process after overcoming technical hurdles like complex epitaxial growth, wafer warpage and breakage, and stringent cross-contamination control.

TSMC, which held its GaN processes to extremely high reliability standards from the start, passed both JEDEC and MIL-STD reliability certifications to ensure robust operations in high-temperature, high-voltage and high-humidity environments. It also warranted yield and defect control through the implementation of fault detection and classification systems and big data driven engineering analysis.

The Navitas factor

Nearly a week after GF acquired TSMC’s GaN fabrication technology, Navitas Semiconductor, one of the largest vendors of GaN chips, announced a GaN foundry partnership with GF in which development work is set for early 2026 and production of GaN semiconductors is expected to begin later in the year.

Navitas’ deep symbiosis with TSMC exemplified the “fabless + foundry” collaboration model in the GaN space. The Torrance, California-based, startup’s breakthrough came in 2017, when its GaNFast power ICs manufactured by TSMC entered volume production. These GaN devices were incorporated into fast chargers of major consumer brands like Anker, Lenovo and Xiaomi.

Since then, Navitas has been considered a super customer and a model reference case for TSMC’s GaN fab business. Navitas, currently TSMC’s largest GaN customer, will utilize GF’s high-voltage GaN-on-Si technology in its Burlington, Vermont, facility, which will qualify for TSMC’s 650-V and 80-V GaN technology.

The high-voltage 650-V platform serves power adapters, motor drives and photovoltaic inverters—all applications with stringent energy-efficiency requirements. On the other hand, mid-voltage 80-V manufacturing nodes are targeted at power systems in servers and laptops.

A U.S. pathway for GaN?

It is worth mentioning that soon after TSMC’s announcement to exit GaN manufacturing, media reports suggested that Navitas would shift its production to Taiwan-based Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corp. (PSMC) over the next 12–24 months. Both TSMC and PSMC use the simpler and more cost-effective GaN-on-Si technology.

Navitas, which accounts for nearly half of TSMC’s GaN wafers, pivoting to GF raises a fundamental question: Is this all about moving the supply chain tilt for a crucial semiconductor technology to U.S. soil? Is it about building a resilient manufacturing footprint in the U.S. for a technology that serves high-power applications spanning AI data centers, EVs and grid infrastructures?

At a time when traditional CMOS-based silicon solutions are hitting performance limitations in power systems, GaN is emerging as a high-voltage technology of choice for its higher efficiency, power density and compactness while serving sleek smartphones to massive data centers. GaN semiconductors hit the sweet spot for some of the toughest power system requirements with high voltage, high frequency and thermal robustness.

The news of the strategic partnership with TSMC coincides with GF receiving an additional $9.5 million in federal funding from the U.S. government to boost its efforts in the GaN fabrication space. According to industry reports, GF has so far received more than $80 million from the U.S. government for its GaN manufacturing-related efforts.

GF’s GaN game

With the TSMC deal, GF is bolstering its GaN-on-Si technology in general, and its PDK readiness and manufacturing yields in particular. GF is currently developing a 200-mm GaN-on-Si manufacturing capability in its fab in Vermont.

The IP collaboration with TSMC will also accelerate GF’s ecosystem capacity. Moreover, it will bring GF more production wins through a tie-up with the Taiwanese mega fab. In short, TSMC’s proven process IP will boost GF’s GaN fabrication offerings and its manufacturing scale.

Meanwhile, GF continues to add new tools, equipment and prototyping capabilities to its GaN IP portfolio and reliability testing. Last year, the pure-play fab acquired Tagore Technology’s GaN IP portfolio to reinforce its commitment to large-scale manufacturing of this wide bandgap technology.

GF, known for its strategic approach to semiconductor manufacturing, is clearly aiming to become a U.S. hub for GaN manufacturing. Furthermore, GF would most likely aim to capture the majority of TSMC’s current wafer business, especially from GaN semiconductor vendors based in the U.S.

While TSMC plans to phase out its GaN wafer operations by July 31, 2027, the year 2026 will likely witness a fundamental shift in the GaN device manufacturing landscape. We will also clearly see the amount of leverage that cost-competitive GaN fabs in China have in this rapidly emerging technology domain.

A GaN power reshuffle

When TSMC announced its exit from the GaN fab business in the summer this year, industry watchers pondered several factors, and they all made sense. Intense pressure from rival fabs in China, lower profit margins and TSMC’s bid to shift resources from legacy technologies to meet growing demand for technologies like Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate made headlines.

However, TSMC, thoroughly strategic in what it does, has not been known as a quitter in its more than three decades of technology journey. So, was handing over this critical technology to the U.S. part of the arrangements with the U.S. technology establishment all along? Or, at least, was it one of the several factors?

Nevertheless, GF’s strategic partnership with TSMC will have a significant impact on the larger GaN semiconductor business. This technology consolidation could also help address many of the challenges presently confronting GaN wafers and their manufacturability at scale.

See also:

TSMC, GlobalFoundries Reach ‘Record’ Settlement

TSMC Packs Up GaN To Focus on Advanced Packaging

GF Targets $1 Billion Silicon Photonics Revenue with AMF Acquisition

data.title: DARPA Quantum Benchmarking Taps Canadian Firms
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Two Canadian quantum computing companies will advance to Stage B of the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) Quantum Benchmarking Initiative (QBI).

Vancouver-based Photonic Inc. will be assessed on the plausibility of their R&D roadmap to realize industrial-scale quantum performance using the company’s Entanglement First architecture, while Xanadu Quantum Technologies Inc. of Toronto will present its fault tolerant, utility-scale quantum computer architecture for assessment.

The aim of DARPA’s QBI is to determine which quantum computing approach will reach utility-scale, and which modalities can deliver value on realistic budgets and real-world timelines. Stage B involves an intensive, year-long examination of the participants’ comprehensive development, scaling and cost control strategies.

Photonic’s selection for this stage follows on its successful delivery of a concept for a utility-scale quantum computer (USQC) based on optically linked silicon spin qubits in Stage A. In an interview with EE Times, Stephanie Simmons, the company’s founder and chief quantum officer, said Stage A, which Photonic entered with many other companies with different quantum modalities, was intense. “Across all these modalities, these people are really smart,” she said. “The experience for DARPA phase A for us has been very positive.”

Simmons said Stage A provided Photonic with the opportunity to demonstrate the promise of its architectural approach to solve the problem of scaling beyond single nodes, which is one of quantum computing’s formidable challenges.

Stage B shifts the focus from the underlying theory to the practical path to building a scalable quantum computer—the QBI is designed to verify and validate if any quantum computing approach can achieve utility-scale operation, which means its computational value exceeds its cost by the year 2033.

“Our effort has always been with the end goal utility in mind,” Simmons said, adding that Photonic is offering the newest modality in the DARPA race—all the other teams that participated are modalities that were invented in 2001 or earlier. “We’re the only new kid on the block from an architecture standpoint.”

Simmons noted that DARPA has shifted the goal posts by demanding provable utility, as well as good unit economics and performance, not just logical error rates.

A year ago, Photonic announced it received access to the advanced fiber-optic network of one of Canada’s major telcos so it can test its quantum technologies and real-world applications.

Stage C is where DARPA accelerates approved participants through the injection of non-dilutive capital revenue, which Simmons noted would be the biggest revenues in the world for quantum unless other sources arose in the intervening years. “It’s not through raising in the public markets,” she said. “It is a revenue source to accelerate your efforts to achieve the vision of a whole new industry.”

Xanada, meanwhile, is going to the public market to raise funds. The company’s announcement that it was selected for Stage B of the DARPA QBI came on the heels of it announcing an IPO.

The company has entered a “definitive business combination” agreement with a newly formed Crane Harbor Acquisition Corp., a publicly traded special purpose acquisition company to create what Xanadu said will be the first and only publicly traded pure play photonic quantum computing company. Upon closing of the transaction, shares of the combined company are expected to trade on the NASDAQ and the Toronto Stock Exchange.

Xanadu said the transaction values the company at a pre-money rollover equity value of $3 billion and the combined company at a pro forma enterprise value of approximately $3.1 billion, with a pro forma market capitalization of $3.6 billion. The combined company is expected to receive approximately $225 million from Crane Harbor’s trust account, assuming no redemptions by Crane Harbor’s public stockholders, as well as $275 million in gross proceeds from a group of strategic and institutional investors.

Xanadu’s photonic quantum computing architecture is setting itself apart from traditional quantum systems by offering minimal cooling requirements due to room-temperature operations, enhanced qubit connectivity that enables more efficient fault tolerant operations, and a modular, networkable design.

The company sees these characteristics as critical for achieving practical quantum computing, which is in line with the goals of the DARPA QBI. Its latest photonic quantum computer, Aurora, serves as a proof-of-concept for this approach and provides the fundamental building blocks needed for large-scale, error-corrected quantum computing.

Christian Weedbrook, CEO of Xanadu, told EE Times that Stage A was a positive experience and allowed the company to showcase the company’s photonic approach for USQC and its performance targets. “Our experience has been very positive. We communicated our vision well.”

He said Stage B of DARPA’s QBI has amassed a very strong test and evaluation team. “That is going to rigorously put our plans to the test. This kind of feedback is highly valuable in science and engineering, particularly when delivering on something as revolutionary as a utility-scale quantum computer.”

Stage B gives Xanadu the opportunity to dive even deeper its plans to build a USQC, Weedbrook added. “It is important for us to share our trajectory with entities such as DARPA. They have dedicated substantial resources toward building a strong test and evaluation effort, which is essential to developing their understanding of what it’s going to take to reach utility-scale.”

The devil is in the details, he said, but Xanadu is confident in its approach. “We’re full steam ahead on executing against it. We are optimistic about our chances of getting into Stage C.”

The selection of Photonic and Xanadu for participation in Stage B of the DARPA QBI comes on the heels of Canada’s federal government allocating CDN $334 million (U.S. $238 million) for quantum technologies in its latest budget.

Canada’s quantum capabilities are spread across the country, with a significant footprint in the province of Quebec, thanks to IBM’s quantum computer in Bromont, and the Nano and Quantum Semiconductors Laboratory at Polytechnique Montréal.

Bromont will soon be home to an industrial-grade quantum fabrication facility co-developed by Montreal’s Anyon Systems Inc. and the MiQro Innovation Collaborative Centre. The facility was made possible by provincial and federal government funding and is expected to be operational in 2026.

data.title: Applied Materials, BESI Push Die-to-Wafer Hybrid Bonding Toward High-Volume Manufacturing
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

At SEMICON Europa this week, Applied Materials and BE Semiconductor Industries (BESI) gathered a small group of journalists for a press briefing on what the companies describe as the industry’s first high-volume–ready die-to-wafer hybrid bonding platform. The partners spent five years developing the integrated Kinex system, which is now being adopted in production by logic, memory and OSAT customers.

Hybrid bonding has been discussed for years as a way to break through scaling limits. What is striking, according to the two speakers, is how rapidly demand is accelerating—and how much engineering complexity is still involved.

Increasing interest in hybrid bonding

“Moore’s Law is slowing,” said Chris Scanlan, senior VP of technology at BESI. “Transistor density is still increasing, but at a slower rate. And at the same time, designers are hitting the reticle-size limit. For many high-performance chips, you simply have to split the die and put it back together using advanced packaging.”

That is the essence of the chiplet trend. Instead of a monolithic SoC on the most advanced process node, designers now mix dies from different nodes and stitch them together at extremely fine pitches. But as Scanlan emphasized, “once you split the die, you need an interconnect technology with very high density, very low loss, and very tight pitch. That technology is hybrid bonding.”

Hybrid bonding joins dies or wafers through direct dielectric-to-dielectric and copper-to-copper interfaces. That eliminates solder bumps and the organic dielectric layers that limit performance and power efficiency.

Gaurav Mehta, director of product marketing for Kinex at Applied Materials, highlighted the drivers. “We’re expecting to see future package sizes expand by 9× and silicon areas increase by 600× in next-generation AI accelerators,” he said. “Some of these packages will have more than 400 dies. Achieving the required bandwidth and latency means shrinking the interconnect gap and packing in more I/Os—upwards of a million I/Os per square millimeter. Hybrid bonding is what enables that.”

Compared to micro-bumps, hybrid bonding reduces thermal resistance, lowers power, cuts latency and shrinks footprint. Applied Materials said the efficiency gains can reach 10× on certain architectures. But despite its promise, the technology is not fully mature. “It’s fair to say this is not a mature technology yet,” Mehta acknowledged. “It still is in its ramp stages. But there are products that have been running in high volume for two or three years, and adoption is increasing.”

Wafer-to-wafer hybrid bonding has been used for close to a decade in memory and image sensors, where die sizes match. “Wafer-to-wafer works when your dies are the same size and your yields line up,” Mehta explained. “But for chiplets, where you may have five different functions, different nodes, and different die sizes, wafer-to-wafer is not practical. Die-to-wafer lets you bond different chiplets coming from different nodes in one planar bonding step.”

This is why die-to-wafer is the key to multi-chiplet packages used in GPUs, CPUs, AI accelerators and upcoming glass-substrate–based modules. And because every incoming die has its own bin, speed grade and quality level, die-level flexibility is essential.

A fully integrated hybrid bonding line

What Applied Materials and BESI said they built together is an integrated line covering every step of the die-to-wafer hybrid bonding flow: surface preparation, plasma activation, cleaning, metrology, alignment and bonding. “This is the first high-volume die-to-wafer hybrid bonding system,” Scanlan said. “It’s a very compact machine, but extremely sophisticated.”

He explained the sequence: Dies arrive on a film frame, already singulated. The target wafer and component wafer are loaded through separate ports. The system uses a multi-stage metrology and vision stack to determine the position of each die, measure alignment to fiducials and correct placement before contact.

“We have to achieve 100-nm alignment, at three sigma,” Scanlan said. “And once the die touches the wafer, Van der Waals forces lock it in. There’s no self-alignment like in flip-chip. So, we have to be extremely precise in the initial placement.”

With today’s Kinex system, customers are achieving 1,600 die placements per hour in high-volume production—up to 2,000 depending on process, according to Scanlan. The platform is scalable: up to six bonder modules can be integrated.

The companies stressed that the single-wafer integrated flow is a key differentiator. Batch surface-prep systems create long “queue times,” where wafers sit after activation, waiting for bonding—and surfaces degrade. In a standalone factory configuration, this queue can reach 13 hours.

“Kinex shortens the readiness window to minutes,” Mehta said. “The system prepares material only when the bonder is ready. If you prepare surfaces too early, you get yield loss and contamination issues. With Kinex, queue-time degradation is cut by roughly 10×.”

Another advantage: eliminating fab-floor moves. Instead of shuttling wafers among multiple tools—clean, plasma, bond, metrology—the entire process stays within a class-one enclosure. Mehta said customers see significantly lower particle-related defects and higher yields as a result.

Customers also increasingly want automated die-bin matching—not all chiplets in a module will come from “bin 1.” Kinex ingests wafer maps and matches dies according to customer-specified rules, reducing manual engineering overhead and scrap, according to the speakers.

Why customization matters

Hybrid bonding is drawing intense interest, Scanlan said, pointing out that the supply side is reacting. “Everybody is developing a hybrid bonder. Japanese players, Korean players, Chinese players, European players—all of them.”

But he argued that Applied and BESI hold an advantage through their joint Center of Excellence (COE) in Singapore. “We get the first look at many of these new applications,” he said. “Customers want to do evaluations and engineering builds, and we can support all of that. We’ve been expanding our COE so we can co-optimize not just the bonding step but what comes before and after.”

The COE includes a range of tools for process co-optimization, allowing customers to tune the entire flow, including warpage engineering. “Warped incoming material is a reality,” Scanlan said. “We know how to manage it on the bonder, but we also work with customers on wafer-level warpage engineering.”

The next-generation hybrid bonder will launch in 2026 with notable improvements, according to Scanlan. “With this upgrade, we’re trying to achieve 50-nm accuracy or better,” he said. “That allows finer pitches for higher-bandwidth chip-to-chip connections. And at the same time, we’re increasing throughput.”

Memory customers—who bond 16-20 dies per package—prioritize throughput more than pitch, and the new architecture lets them trade accuracy for speed. A more radical architectural redesign is already underway for the generation after that, pushing below 25-nm alignment accuracy while increasing unit-per-hour rates further. “We have to do both—improve accuracy and improve productivity,” Scanlan said. “This is our roadmap.”

As both speakers emphasized, hybrid bonding is not yet “plug-and-play.” Yields vary, surfaces are hypersensitive to contamination and metrology requirements keep climbing. But the trajectory is clear. The shift from monolithic SoCs to multi-chiplet architecture is accelerating. AI workloads are pushing package sizes and I/O counts to unprecedented scales. And the economics of mixing process nodes is too powerful to ignore.

The Applied–BESI Kinex system is marketed as one of the first hybrid bonding platforms proven in high-volume production, and both companies are now racing to extend its accuracy, speed and flexibility.

“Hybrid bonding is becoming a go-to technology,” Mehta said. “The application space is diverse, and we’re just at the beginning.”

See also: At SEMICON West in Phoenix, Ariz., last month, EE Times caught up with Prabu Raja, president of the Semiconductor Products Group at Applied Materials. You can see what he said in this video interview taken at the show in October 2025, in which he talks about how the need for energy efficient compute means that every angstrom counts in semiconductor manufacturing. Watch the video interview here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q26z8knh7N8

data.title: AI to Drive Surge in Memory Prices Through 2026
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The expansion of AI will push memory prices up by as much as 20% next year, even after soaring in 2025, according to market research firm Counterpoint.

Memory prices are likely to rise 30% in Q4 2025 and possibly another 20% early next year, following 50% price increase this year, according to the company.

One of the key issues is a dearth of legacy LPDDR4 memory after suppliers like SK Hynix and Micron shifted output to more advanced high-bandwidth memory (HBM) to meet AI demand, Counterpoint said in a research report.

Spot-price imbalances are occurring, with DDR5 for servers and PCs trading at around $1.50 per gigabit, while older DDR4 used in consumer electronics fetches $2.10—higher than that of even advanced HBM3e, which is hovering around $1.70, the report said. 

Counterpoint, which forecasts DRAM production increases exceeding 20% in 2026, says suppliers are racing to meet demand.

“Samsung may reallocate its expanding 1C process, SK Hynix is pushing production and raising sales targets, CXMT may surprise on the upside and Micron, typically disciplined on return on investment, is unlikely to hold back,” Counterpoint Research Director MS Hwang said in the report. “The bigger risk on the horizon is with advanced memory as Nvidia’s recent pivot to LPDDR means it is a customer on the scale of a major smartphone maker—a seismic shift for the supply chain which can’t easily absorb this scale of demand.”

Record performance

SK Hynix, the world’s largest HBM supplier by sales, reported its highest quarterly performance in its most recent earnings results in October, driven by soaring prices of DRAM and NAND, as well as increasing shipments of HBM products for AI servers.

SK Hynix briefly eclipsed Samsung this year as the world’s largest memory maker on the strength of SK Hynix’s HBM sales. During the second half of 2025, Samsung regained its title as the leader in overall sales revenue.

“As the AI market rapidly shifts toward inference-driven workloads, there is growing interest in distributing computational loads of AI servers across broader infrastructures such as general servers,” SK Hynix said in its October earnings statement. “This trend is expected to further expand demand across the entire memory portfolio, including high-performance DDR5 and eSSD.”

The company plans to speed migration to its most advanced 1c DRAM process, a 10-nm node, that is in production. The shift toward the finer geometry will allow the company to establish a “full” DRAM lineup across server, mobile and graphics applications, according to SK Hynix.

The company’s leading HBM4 line will start shipping in the fourth quarter this year.

Amid surging demand for AI memory, SK Hynix said it has “secured full customer demand” for its DRAM and NAND production next year.

Spreading pain

The spike in memory prices is initially impacting smartphone makers, but others in the electronics ecosystem are at risk, according to Counterpoint.

“The pain could spread across the broader smartphone and consumer electronics ecosystem,” said Counterpoint Senior Analyst Ivan Lam in the report. “We are talking here about big increases to smartphone BoMs—upwards of 25% in the case of some models—across the key mid-to-high-end segments, eating into margins or affecting growth. It will probably be both.”

The industry faces constrained capacity and surging prices, which will force “painful” trade-offs for suppliers and manufacturers alike, the report said.

data.title: Ainekko Buys Esperanto Hardware IP, Open-Sources It
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Open-source AI hardware and software startup Ainekko has acquired the IP and selected assets of AI chip startup Esperanto Technologies, including its chip designs, software tooling, and development framework. Ainekko plans to open-source Esperanto’s production-grade many-core RISC-V architecture, including RTL, reference designs and development tools.

Ainekko started as a software layer for inference and distributed inference, but the founders quickly realized that without a co-optimized hardware backend, improvements to software were limited, Ainekko co-founder Tanya Dadasheva told EE Times.

“We always wanted flexibility,” Dadasheva said. “We believe [hardware] should be understandable by people in the open-source [software] ecosystem, they should be able to reason about it, and it should be flexible enough for people to do stuff with it.”

Esperanto, founded in 2014, had released a 1088-core RISC-V chip for hyperscale data centers’ recommendation workloads in 2021. The company pivoted toward HPC and generative AI in 2023, but ultimately found the hyperscale market difficult to break into and suffered from the loss of its European design team.

Dadasheva had worked with Esperanto in her previous role at Almaz Capital and had initially intended for Ainekko to partner with Esperanto, running Ainekko’s software stack on Esperanto hardware. When it became clear Esperanto wasn’t sustainable as a long-term partner, Ainekko decided acquiring the IP would be the best course of action.

Ainekko’s team has been working with Esperanto’s chips for about six months, Dadasheva said, and they have llama.cpp up and running, as well as the tinygrad framework. Some of Esperanto’s current-generation many-core chips will be used to bootstrap community initiatives, including FFmpeg work.

“Some people might come up with ideas of how to use it, beyond what we do as a business,” Dadasheva said. “Some people want to run shaders on it and try to do an open GPU type of solution, [for example], but one of the most interesting use cases for this is to use it as a full system-on-chip, without a host, in edge use cases.”

Esperanto had focused on hyperscale data centers, later pivoting slightly toward HPC with its 1000-core design. Ainekko expects new use cases for the technology to be focused on the edge, where power efficiency is critical. The architecture suits most edge AI applications, including industrial, robotics and drones, security systems, and embedded devices of all kinds.

“We are not trying to go for HPC and training, that is out of the question, but even in the inference space we don’t want to go for the data center, at least not for the next few years,” Dadasheva said.

Esperanto’s many-core RISC-V design made a good AI chip, but it will make an excellent general-purpose compute platform, Ainekko cofounder Roman Shaposhnik told EE Times.

“That’s exactly the match that we have with open-source, because open-source always goes for generality, even at the risk of losing some performance numbers, generality always wins,” he said.

Industrial PCs could be an interesting use case for Ainekko hardware and software, according to Shaposhnik, especially given the sizable total addressable market (TAM). Another area of interest is smart interposers for SSD interfaces which use AI to look for abnormal reads and writes, killing the system if it detects a security breach. No SoCs exist for this use case today, Shaposhnik said.

Silicon plans

Ainekko is making plans for its own first silicon. The company is planning a tapeout with just eight Esperanto cores (a single Esperanto “neighborhood” – Esperanto’s own design was 1088 cores), which it plans to attach to 16 MB of MRAM from startup Veevx (Veevx cofounder and CEO, Broadcom veteran Doug Smith, is also Ainekko’s third cofounder). This tapeout will go onto an imminent TSMC shuttle wafer.

Next on the roadmap will be an intermediate-size design in the region of 256 cores, Dadasheva said, coming closer in performance to the quad-Arm-Cortex-A76 processor on the Raspberry Pi 5, but more optimized for AI inference.

“We are not afraid to open [the hardware IP] to the community, but as a business, we want to offer something like a Raspberry Pi 5 that could be used for prototyping,” Dadasheva said.

Cloud access to the company’s existing Esperanto cards is already available in order to help potential users evaluate how many cores they need, amongst other design parameters. Esperanto’s emulator and software stack has already been open-sourced, and the community is already getting comfortable working with these technologies, Dadasheva said.

“We have seen a lot of interest from academia to take Esperanto cores and start experimenting with them, not necessarily taping them out, but putting them on an FPGA and reasoning about them,” Dadasheva said. “If it suddenly takes off and people tape it out, that’s a really good thing because it will increase our software footprint.”

Anyone taping out Esperanto-based hardware would not necessarily need to use Ainekko software, but the company’s stack will be optimized directly for the hardware IP and the two will evolve together, Dadasheva said. Concerns about working with startup IP are mitigated by showing Esperanto’s existing silicon and allowing complete access to inspect and download the code, she added. There are also plans afoot to make the hardware IP available via embedded chiplet platform ZeroASIC, she said.

Ainekko’s hardware IP and software has been open-sourced under the AI Foundry brand, which is not currently a foundation, but it could be, Dadasheva said. Discussions about a potential foundation will continue into next year.

Ainekko plans to eventually provide ASIC design services to potential customers. There are also possibilities for hybrid relationships with companies that are taping out their own chips, including sales partnerships, Shaposhnik said.

“Nobody has really tried to run an open-source RTL design fully in the open,” Shaposhnik said. “But we’re at the point where it will be possible to collaborate on smaller designs on higher-level nodes. It’s a big question mark; prerequisites to that obviously include our actual RTL being available in the open, but also tools, at least on the front end of the process for design and emulation.”

data.title: D-Wave Among Few Ramping Quantum Computer Sales
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

D-Wave chief development officer Trevor Lanting told EE Times in an exclusive interview that the company sees sales of quantum computing systems increasing. D-Wave and IBM are the only quantum companies in that category so far.

D-Wave stole a lead on rivals by being the first to introduce its quantum-annealing tech, with a shorter path to commercialization. Now D-Wave is doing the hard part: the gate-model devices that it and competitors may need about seven years to bring to production.

In the meantime, D-Wave is adding sales of hardware to its bread-and-butter quantum-as-a-service business.

“Over the last year, there’s been a growing interest in on-premises quantum infrastructure and quantum-computing systems,” Lanting said. “At the end of 2024, we closed a sale of our Advantage system to the Jülich Supercomputing Center, and we deployed that system earlier in 2025.”

The aim is to connect the quantum system to Europe’s Jupiter exascale computer, enabling breakthroughs in AI and quantum optimization. The project would be the world’s first coupling of an annealing quantum computer with an exascale supercomputer. D-Wave has also installed systems at the University of Southern California’s Information Sciences Institute; in Huntsville, Alabama, with Davidson Technologies; and in Italy for the Q-Alliance, according to Murray Thom, D-Wave’s VP of quantum technology evangelism.

“In Davidson’s case, they want to work on a set of important defense applications, and you need a specific IT context to do that,” Thom said. “They want to set that up to enable the defense industry to be able to take advantage of the technology. In the case of the Q-Alliance, the Italian government wants to take an advanced position in the knowledge economy, and that is about workforce upskilling, advanced research and enabling companies to get the productivity, efficiency and agility that quantum-computing technology is providing today.”

D-Wave’s annealing quantum computing tech is designed specifically to solve optimization problems by using quantum mechanics to find the lowest energy state of a system. The quantum computer starts in a superposition of all possible solutions and evolves toward a ground state, which represents the optimal solution.

In March, D-Wave claimed to be first in quantum supremacy when it published a peer-reviewed article in Science demonstrating how its annealing quantum computer performed in minutes magnetic materials simulations that would have taken nearly one million years and more than the world’s annual electricity supply to solve using the Frontier supercomputer at the Department of Energy’s Oak Ridge National Laboratory.

Gate-model tech

Earlier this year, D-Wave said it would pivot to development of the more challenging gate-model technology that IBM, Google, Rigetti Computing and Quantinuum have been working on.

D-Wave saw no commercially viable path toward a gate-model tech 15-plus years ago, but two critical innovations in the intervening years changed the company’s thinking. The first is error-correction methodology, finding a way to do encoding and error correction with gate-model architectures. The second change is fundamental innovation in qubit design.

“We see a path toward a large-scale gate-model technology,” Lanting noted. “Based on superconducting approaches, our gate-model qubits are our so-called fluxonium qubits. They’re very similar to the qubits that are in our annealing processor. Parametrically a little bit different, but we can adapt the electronics, the shielding, the cryogenic control and the fabrication that we’ve built up for the annealing technology. We see a lot of fundamental synergy between the two technology development efforts.”

All the quantum industry’s gate-model efforts will probably take somewhere from seven to 15 years of sophisticated R&D to reach a scale where the work yields commercial applications, Thom said.

“Our customers want us to help make sense for them on that spectrum of applications between what’s immediately available, what’s transformative and revolutionary now and in the future, what’s next,” Thom noted. “With gate model, you’re not targeting the same applications. It’s not going to be efficient for optimization, but if you want to do molecular-simulation calculations, that’s the only way to do it.”

During 2025, D-Wave had more than 100 revenue-generating customers, including about 20 in the Forbes Global 2,000. The company sells quantum services via its Leap Cloud Platform to companies like MasterCard, Ford and Volkswagen.

SkyWater partnership

D-Wave plans to continue its engagement with SkyWater as its chip foundry for both annealing and gate-based quantum processors, Lanting said.

“The qubits are manufactured and embedded in this multi-layer fabrication stack,” Lanting noted. “Skywater makes the chip that’s at the core of the system, a large superconducting integrated circuit. A key to our success in getting to the 5000-qubit scale is that we have on-chip digital analog converters. We can control a very large number of these DACs with a very small number of lines because we have on-chip demultiplexing. A good chunk of the complexity is involved with the digital control, but all the key bits are also manufactured and embedded at the same time within the same integrated stack.”

A critical part of D-Wave’s gate-model development is the scalable control circuitry, Lanting added.

“That’s definitely something we will be leveraging Skywater to produce,” he noted. “Skywater remains critical to that development program.”

data.title: Crypto Mining ASIC Goes Deep Sub-Threshold On 3 nm
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Japanese crypto-mining ASIC company Triple-1 is about to tape out its third-generation Bitcoin-mining chip, Kamikaze III, built on TSMC 3-nm process technology. Kamikaze III will offer power efficiency of 10.45 J/TH, thanks to proprietary techniques that enable sub-threshold operation, and the company plans to use the same technology in a forthcoming AI accelerator chip, Triple-1 president and CEO Takuya Yamaguchi told EE Times in an exclusive interview.

Yamaguchi founded Triple-1 in 2016 and the company launched its first generation Kamikaze crypto-mining chip in 2018 on TSMC 7 nm. A second-generation crypto-mining chip was launched in 2020 alongside a test chip for an in-house AI accelerator, both on TSMC 5 nm.

The first generation of the Kamikaze chip was launched in 2018. Crypto-mining chips became a difficult market, Yamaguchi said, because of the downturn in cryptocurrencies’ value. Triple-1 started working with the leading Japanese utility Tepco (Tokyo Electric Power Company) in 2021.

Following the Fukushima nuclear disaster, Japanese utilities invested heavily in renewable energy generation—primarily solar—but renewables can be unpredictable as they are often weather-dependent. Excess energy supply can either be stored or wasted, and in 2018, it was generally wasted as battery costs were high, Yamaguchi said.

Triple-1’s collaboration with Tepco led the company down two paths: the company teamed up with Tepco subsidiary Agile Energy to deploy crypto-mining servers at the edge, close to where the energy is generated but used only when there is an energy surplus, and simultaneously developed a solid-state battery product.

“We do not only focus on semiconductor design,” Yamaguchi said. “As we look at the market, everything is strongly connected: power generation, infrastructure and transmission, energy storage and supply—they are all linked to the data center.”

Energy storage

“Fluctuating energy supply is a huge problem for all electricity companies in the world,” Yamaguchi added. “High-performance batteries which are safe and cheap will be critical in the future for a safer, more stable electricity market.”

The company has a separate in-house team working on solid-state battery development. Triple-1’s solid state battery achieves energy density above 280 Wh/kg.

“We have a deep discussion with not just Tokyo Electric, but also outside of Japanese electricity companies and governments to utilize this battery,” Yamaguchi said.

Japan’s electricity grid, unlike Western “mesh” grids, has a “fishbone” structure, with side lines coming out of a main trunk. Fishbone grids in particular suffer from congestion, but both grid topologies have thinner electricity cables in suburban areas, which are not suitable for demand on the grid and transmission from suburban solar installations.

“Investment into this cable is quite expensive for electricity companies,” he said. “To store energy in the local area, or to run the data center [on surplus energy], can add value .… because of that, there is a good fit to collaborate with these players.”

Triple-1 wants to bring its battery and its edge crypto-mining servers to governments and utilities in Asia and around the world.

Power efficiency

Crypto-mining server deployment at the edge relies on power efficiency. Triple-1 achieves this by working on the most advanced process nodes available, as well as employing proprietary sub-threshold design techniques.

Kamikaze III will be built on TSMC 3 nm. Being Japanese gives Triple-1 an advantage as the company is freely able to access these advanced process nodes (a majority of the crypto ASIC market share is held by Bitmain, a Chinese company, and its affiliate Sophgo, who have been denied access to TSMC since Sophgo chips were found in Huawei equipment).  

“Triple-1 is the only player outside of China who has succeeded in developing this kind of chip,” Yamaguchi said. “Even the big U.S. players couldn’t make it happen. So, we have a certain uniqueness in the market.”

Crypto-mining chips are a popular choice for foundries as a “pipe-cleaner,” the first products to be produced using a new process node. This is partly because crypto-mining chips do not have any high-speed interface IP, codec IP or SRAM, which need to be verified and tested separately. This type of chip has a relatively simple architecture with a single clock. They are also relatively small, high-yield, high-volume parts. This helps companies like Triple-1 get early access to new process nodes.

Triple-1 plans to collaborate with fledgeling Japanese foundry Rapidus on its advanced process nodes in the next couple of years, Yamaguchi said, as a way to offer a Japan-only supply chain to customers as an option.

Sub-threshold techniques

Triple-1 has used sub-threshold operation in multiple generations of its crypto-mining chips. For Kamikaze III, Triple-1’s target operating point is 0.26 V.

Sub-threshold design is beyond the limits of automated EDA tools today. Design is therefore enabled by Triple-1’s additional characterization of TSMC’s PDK, custom cell design and manual routing, which together are a significant part of the company’s IP.

“The strength of our technology lies in its ability to minimize power consumption and cost while controlling risk,” Yamaguchi said. “After ensuring chip functionality, our highest priority is low power consumption, followed by cost, and finally, temperature. We will select air cooling for the final system if the thermal requirements are not extremely strict. If the thermal requirements are exceptionally demanding, we will opt for liquid cooling to ensure the chip always operates correctly.”

Other temperature mitigation techniques include low-power modes like sleep and deep sleep modes, which can expand the chip’s environmental temperature range. Specific operating modes can be introduced during the design phase if the likely environmental temperature profile is known. Advanced techniques, like software-cooperative design where temperature is sensed and fed back to enable dynamic switching of operating modes, are also possible.

Sub-threshold has become a viable technique as crypto-mining chips have reduced in size (since larger chips restrict clock speed at sub-threshold voltages), Yamaguchi said, noting that multiple small chips are used on each board.

Next gen AI

Could Triple-1’s sub-threshold techniques be applied to future AI chips? And could AI chips be deployed at the edge, in or near solar farms, to generate LLM tokens with surplus energy?

In fact, many crypto-mining companies are already changing their focus to AI, Yamaguchi said. (For example, cloud company IREN is transitioning from a crypto-mining company to an AI cloud company, and recently signed a $9.7 billion deal with Microsoft.)

“[Crypto-mining] players are working to start collaborating with U.S. hyperscalers,” Yamaguchi said. “There’s one critical reason: the electricity contract [crypto-mining companies] have with local electricity companies.”

Today, 10-12 GW of capacity, or about 15 % of the entire data center industry’s power footprint, is used to power crypto mining. These companies have already negotiated favorable prices from their electricity suppliers, which look very attractive to AI companies, Yamaguchi said.

Triple-1’s existing relationships with crypto mining companies, combined with its distributed computing approach and access to an otherwise overlooked source of power, could be a competitive advantage for future engagements with AI companies, he added.

“We are focusing at this moment on the mining side, not AI, but once the market requires it, we can easily shift to AI,” Yamaguchi said. “Suburban data centers are in the near future for AI, not a centralized GPU-based hyperscale data center. To make AI services available to the entire country, we need to deploy locally.”

Triple-1 built a 5-nm AI accelerator test chip, Goku, in 2020. This chip used an in-house designed training NPU, achieving 10 TFLOPS/W at FP16, but did not use the company’s sub-threshold design expertise. However, these technologies could be combined for a future AI chip, especially if the apparent synergy between sub-threshold’s reliability and AI inference’s lower precision requirements could be exploited.

For future applications like AI chips, sub-threshold operation could be applied only to certain blocks on the chip to mitigate issues with clock speed, Yamaguchi said.

Another issue for sub-threshold is SRAM, which is notoriously unstable at low voltage. Kamikaze crypto-mining chips do not use SRAM, in part because use of very advanced process nodes meant SRAM had not been fully characterized for the target process node. In any case, this problem has been greatly reduced for modern finFETs, Yamaguchi said, noting that the company does not expect this to be an issue for future sub-threshold designs, including SRAM.

Challenges for a future Triple-1 AI chip include interconnect. Like AI chips, crypto-mining chips are many-core, though the cores work independently to perform the same hash calculation. This is the opposite of an AI accelerator where cores need to work together on a single, large problem. Thus, while core-to-core and chip-to-chip interconnect is not crucial for crypto-mining, it would be for an AI accelerator.

Triple-1 has commercial edge AI inference chips on its roadmap, Yamaguchi said, but whether AI token generation makes sense for distributed computing is still under discussion.

In the mean time, Triple-1 will seek to commercialize its distributed computing infrastructure concept with governments and utilities in Asia and further afield, either by selling chips or by working with partners to provide servers or higher-level solutions, Yamaguchi said.

data.title: GF Targets $1 Billion Silicon Photonics Revenue with AMF Acquisition
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

GlobalFoundries (GF) today announced the acquisition of Advanced Micro Foundry (AMF), a silicon photonics foundry based in Singapore, claiming this makes GF the world’s largest pure-play silicon photonics foundry. GF said AMF will add over $75 million to its revenue in 2026, and the company expects its annual silicon photonics revenue to be in excess of $1 billion by the end of the decade.

The value of the deal was not disclosed, but GF expects AMF’s manufacturing assets, extensive IP and skilled talent to significantly expand GF’s silicon photonics technology and market. In the official announcement, GF said it is leveraging over 15 years of AMF’s manufacturing expertise to address demands in long-haul optical communications, computing, LiDAR and sensing on AMF’s 200-mm platform in Singapore. The company also has plans to scale to 300 mm as market needs grow, “ensuring reliable global supply for AI datacenters, communications and next-generation applications,” according to GF.

GF said that as it continues to expand its U.S. manufacturing capacity for silicon photonics in New York, this acquisition of AMF would accelerate the ramp of its Singapore operation. GF also plans to establish a silicon photonics research and development center of excellence in Singapore, partnering with the Agency for Science, Technology and Research (A*STAR), Singapore’s lead public sector R&D agency, to advance GF’s innovation roadmap by focusing on next-generation materials for ultra-fast data transfer at 400Gbps speed.

AMF is a specialty silicon photonics foundry offering a full spectrum of manufacturing, prototyping and testing services, supported by proprietary technology platforms. It uses CMOS-compatible processes and a proven 200-mm production line, which the company says delivers high-yield, cost-efficient photonic integrated circuits.

For its prototyping services, AMF states it offers prototyping services and multi-wafer project shuttles on multiple platforms—silicon-on-insulator (SOI) as well silicon nitride on SOI—AMF also offers wafer level packaging solutions.

GF CEO Tim Breen said in a LinkedIn post today, “This move fits perfectly with our decade-long strategy in silicon photonics—a technology that’s now critical for powering the data-driven world, from cloud data centers to the physical AI systems at the edge.”

In an official announcement Breen said, “Acquiring AMF enables GF to deliver an expanded, and differentiated, decade-long roadmap for pluggable transceivers and co-packaged optics, while accelerating growth of photonics into adjacent markets such as automotive and quantum computing.” 

Silicon photonics is a must have

Silicon photonics is now widely expected to address some of the limitations in AI data center infrastructure. As traditional copper connections reach their physical limits, silicon photonics has been emerging as a potential breakthrough technology by using light to transfer data between and within data centers, delivering ultra-fast, energy-efficient performance.

“Silicon photonics technology is essential for AI infrastructure,” Breen said. “As data moves faster and workloads grow more complex, the ability to move information with greater speed, precision and power efficiency is now fundamental to AI datacenters and advanced telecom networks.”

The opportunity for optical networking in data center infrastructure. (Source: GlobalFoundries)

The silicon photonics opportunity. (Source: GlobalFoundries)

According to a report released in May 2025 by analyst firm LightCounting, silicon photonics is now a “must have technology.” The report notes that despite many advantages of silicon photonics technology, it took almost a decade for it to make an impact on the optical transceiver market.

“Decisions made by several large companies, including Cisco, Huawei and Intel, helped to accelerate adoption of silicon photonics,” the report said. “We expect that use of linear drive pluggables (LPO) and co-packaged optics (CPO) will double the market share of this technology from 30% in 2025 to 60% in 2030, as illustrated in the figure below. Once again, several large companies, including Broadcom and Nvidia, will be enabling this transition.”

Silicon photonics transceivers market share. (Source: LightCounting).

LightCounting said the transition from pluggable to CPO is an exciting one for the industry, but it is important to set realistic expectations for the adoption of these solutions. Apart from numerous manufacturing challenges and meeting the targets set for lower power consumption, end users must accept CPO as a viable approach for continuing cost reduction.

Its report estimates the market value of optical chips used in transceivers, active optical cables, LPO and CPO shipped last year was a modest $1.7 billion, and silicon photonics chips account for about one third of that. The market is projected to triple in size, exceeding $5 billion in 2030 and the market share of silicon photonics is projected to double.

 “This is a 6× growth and the forecasted market value is a bit more exciting, but hardly enough for companies like TSMC. It must be the long-term strategy that attracts CMOS foundries to silicon photonics,” the report said. “Something along the lines of Intel’s vision for the last two decades: where the optical interconnects, such as CPO, become an essential technology for any complex ASIC to function. This vision may be only a decade away now and this is the right time to focus on silicon photonics. Intel might have been 20 years early, but no large CMOS foundry or ASIC vendor can afford to miss it now.”

See also:

Photonics Chipmakers Race to Production

Can TFLN Make Photonic Compute Competitive?

AI and Chiplets Prominent at TSMC OIP 2025

Ayar Labs and Alchip Unveil Optical I/O Reference DesignSTARLight Ignites Europe’s Silicon Photonics Ambitions: From Lab to Fab at 300mm

data.title: Dutch Clampdown on Nexperia Puts Europe’s Chip Sovereignty Strategy to the Test
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

When the Dutch government invoked an emergency law in late September 2025 to take control of semiconductor maker Nexperia, it signaled a turning point in how Europe intends to defend its technological sovereignty. The action was justified as a matter of national security, but it is reverberating through the semiconductor supply chain—and the automotive industry, in particular, is feeling the tremors.

A rare move under the Goods Availability Act

Nexperia, headquartered in Nijmegen, Netherlands, is one of Europe’s largest producers of legacy chips—the diodes, MOSFETs and logic components that quietly power cars, industrial systems, and consumer electronics. The company, a former NXP Semiconductors unit, has been owned by China’s Wingtech Technology since 2019.

Citing “acute governance shortcomings” and “risks to the continuity and safeguarding of crucial technological capabilities,” the Dutch Ministry of Economic Affairs temporarily seized control of the firm under the Goods Availability Act, a Cold War–era law seldom used since the 1950s. The move suspended CEO Zhang Xuezheng (aka Wingtech Zhang) and granted the government a deciding vote over management decisions.

Beijing responded by curbing exports of components and tooling from Wingtech-controlled facilities in China, raising fears of shortages in Europe’s already-tight automotive supply chain.

The irony is that Nexperia does not make the advanced AI processors that dominate geopolitical headlines. It produces mature, high-volume chips on 8-inch wafers—devices often considered commodities. Yet those parts are the backbone of Europe’s car manufacturing sector, where hundreds of small, validated components come together under strict qualification and safety rules. That is what makes the disruption so serious, said Jo Van Biesebroeck, professor of economics at KU Leuven.

“Changing a supplier in the automotive industry takes time,” he told EE Times in an interview. “Vehicle components are interdependent, so any change has ripple effects. Coordinating sourcing adjustments can take more than six months.”

Even seemingly simple substitutions can set off a cascade of redesigns, retesting and re-certifications. For Tier 1 suppliers and car OEMs already stretched by electrification and software-defined-vehicle projects, the timing could hardly be worse.

According to Van Biesebroeck, automakers usually hedge risk at the corporate level but not at the product level. “OEMs dual-source in order not to be overly reliant on one supplier,” he said. “But at the model level, they often single-source.
